decks.title: Lecture 1 - What Does The Optimizer See?

slides.title: Can we design a stellarator by following gradients?
slides.subtitle: Lecture 1: what the optimizer actually sees
bullets: Equilibrium, Boozer spectrum, and metrics become the design language
Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: PART 1. From field lines to hidden symmetry
bullets: Goal: turn confinement physics into computable objectives
Caveat: geometry freedom creates an inverse problem

slides.title: Metrics are compressed physics
bullets: They are useful only when we know what was compressed

slides.title: Three literature gates shape the workflow
bullets: Precise quasisymmetry: make the Boozer spectrum sparse, then test what remains
Good magnetic surfaces: compare surface-based objectives with island and chaos diagnostics
W7-X validation: reduced neoclassical transport is real progress, but it is one gate in the loop
slides.reference: Refs: Landreman & Paul PRL 128, 035001 (2022); Landreman et al. Phys. Plasmas 28, 092505 (2021); Beidler et al. Nature 596, 221-226 (2021).

slides.title: The optimizer sees artifacts with provenance
bullets: Boundary and profiles create an equilibrium
Spectra and scalars turn physics into objectives
Every scalar needs a validation gate

slides.title: The first object is a surface
bullets: Read the shape as a parameterized boundary
This is a cached educational surface
slides.caption: A visible surface is a design variable, not yet a validated device.
slides.reference: VMEC background: Hirshman & Whitson, Phys. Fluids 26, 3553 (1983); public cases from landreman/vmec_equilibria.

slides.title: W7-X is the reference case to keep in mind
bullets: Use it as experimental motivation
Do not treat the fallback surface as a new W7-X result
slides.caption: The reference case anchors the design discussion without duplicating geometry lectures.
slides.reference: Ref: Beidler et al., Nature 596, 221-226 (2021), reduced neoclassical energy transport in W7-X.

slides.title: Rotational transform is an optimization target
bullets: Plot pitch before optimizing spectra
Watch low-order rational surfaces
slides.caption: A profile can be a constraint, an objective, or a diagnostic.
slides.reference: Ref: Landreman, Medasani & Zhu, Phys. Plasmas 28, 092505 (2021), good surfaces and quasisymmetry together.

slides.title: A small boundary mode can move the objective
bullets: One knob changes QS residual and aspect ratio
This is the gradient intuition for stage 1
slides.caption: The optimizer needs knobs whose effects can be measured.

slides.title: Equilibrium convergence is part of the result
bullets: Record resolution and residuals
Do not compare designs before the solve is trustworthy
Cached mode keeps the teaching path alive

slides.title: The wout file is the first checked artifact
bullets: Inspect dimensions, surfaces, and mode counts
Use public HSX/W7-X files when present
Record what was actually read

slides.title: Demo break: first equilibrium object
bullets: Locate the HSX wout file
Plot surface and iota
Change one boundary-mode proxy
slides.caption: Cached mode first. Repo: https://github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization | Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: PART 2. Boozer coordinates make symmetry measurable
bullets: The spectrum turns geometry into a table
Bad modes become penalties

slides.title: Boozer coordinates make symmetry visible
bullets: Clean bands suggest the intended symmetry
Broken bands warn before the scalar metric
slides.caption: This contour is a teaching diagnostic, not a fresh Boozer transform.

slides.title: The spectrum tells the optimizer what to penalize
bullets: Green modes preserve the intended line
Red modes are symmetry-breaking targets
slides.caption: Good and bad modes are marked explicitly for the classroom scan.
slides.reference: Ref: Landreman & Paul, Phys. Rev. Lett. 128, 035001 (2022), precise quasisymmetry.

slides.title: A bad mode changes the picture immediately
bullets: Perturb one coefficient
Regenerate contour and residual
slides.caption: The exercise is to connect a table entry to a visible field-strength change.

slides.title: Several symmetry targets use the same workflow
bullets: Quasi-axisymmetry: penalize modes off the QA line
Quasi-helical symmetry: keep the QH ridge clean
Quasi-isodynamic: use different metrics but the same artifact discipline

slides.title: Effective ripple is an early transport warning
bullets: Lower is better for this screen
Use it before expensive validation
slides.caption: This cached curve teaches how to read a metric; it is not a new NEO result.
slides.reference: Ref: Beidler et al., Nature 596, 221-226 (2021), effective ripple as a W7-X optimization target.

slides.title: Gradients tell us which knobs matter
bullets: Finite differences are robust for teaching
Autodiff becomes important at scale
slides.caption: The point is sensitivity ranking, not a production derivative.

slides.title: The scalar objective is a negotiation
bullets: Weights encode scientific and engineering priorities

slides.title: Demo break: spectrum and ripple
bullets: Circle bad modes
Change one scalar
Explain what the plot proves
slides.caption: Cached mode first. Repo: https://github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization | Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: Failure mode: optimizing a beautiful plot
bullets: A clean surface is not a validated design
A scalar can hide the wrong physics
A cached figure must say what it is

slides.title: Lecture 1 what to remember
bullets: Optimization starts with a parameterization
A wout file is a scientific artifact
Boozer spectra turn symmetry into penalties
Metrics must stay connected to validation

slides.title: A design loop starts when the artifact can be rerun
bullets: Repo and docs are part of the scientific object

slides.title: APPENDIX. Lecture 1 checks and replacements
bullets: Use this section when a live package succeeds
Keep cached mode as the classroom baseline

slides.title: VMEC file review
bullets: Dimensions: surfaces, modes, field periods
Variables: iota, pressure, boundary coefficients
Provenance: public repo path and hash

slides.title: Boozer file review
bullets: Surface labels: choose the same radial grid
Mode truncation: report m/n limits
Residual: separate symmetry-preserving from breaking modes

slides.title: When cached mode is enough
bullets: Teaching the loop structure
Testing plotting and data plumbing
Preparing exercises with qualitative answers

slides.title: When research mode is needed
bullets: Reporting a numerical value
Comparing HSX and W7-X quantitatively
Changing VMEC or Boozer resolution

slides.title: Research path: VMEC_JAX to Boozer transform
bullets: Start from a verified input or wout file
Run a tiny equilibrium on the lecture machine
Replace cached arrays with documented outputs

slides.title: Metric acceptance checklist
bullets: Cached figure: use for trend and classroom discussion
Real-code run: use for numerical claim after provenance check
STATUS.md record: package, data source, and validation domain

slides.title: Backup figure: HSX surface
bullets: Use if the live surface plot fails
Keep the cached label visible
slides.caption: Fallback image for a short live-demo recovery.

slides.title: Backup figure: Boozer contour
bullets: Use if the transform is slow
Discuss the expected symmetry pattern
slides.caption: Fallback image for reading the contour before scalarizing.

slides.title: Discussion: what did the optimizer see?
bullets: Design variable: boundary modes and profiles
Computed object: equilibrium, spectra, scalar metrics
Decision variable: which scalar deserves trust

slides.title: What to remember
bullets: Keep the scientific object and the computed artifact together
Rerun, perturb, compare, and explain before trusting the optimum
Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: Can we build the field that we optimized?
slides.subtitle: Lecture 2: coils, quadratic flux, and single-stage design
bullets: A plasma boundary is a wish; coils are the contract
Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: PART 1. The coil inverse problem
bullets: Stage 1 gives a target surface
Stage 2 asks hardware to reproduce it

slides.title: Never show a beautiful plasma without its coils
bullets: The engineering contract starts at B dot n

slides.title: Stage 1, stage 2, single stage
bullets: Stage 1: optimize the plasma target
Stage 2: fit coils to that target
Single stage: move plasma and coils together

slides.title: Single-stage changed the controlled object
bullets: Two-stage: choose a plasma boundary first, then ask coils to reproduce it
Fixed-boundary single-stage: boundary and coils move together through a quadratic-flux penalty
Simplified coils: few-coil designs become searchable, but engineering gates still decide viability
slides.reference: Refs: Jorge et al., PPCF 65, 074003 (2023); Jorge, Giuliani & Loizu, arXiv:2406.07830 (2024); Giuliani et al., JCP 459, 111147 (2022).

slides.title: Initial coils define the first mismatch
bullets: Look for scale, symmetry, and crowding
The curves are synthetic teaching coils
slides.caption: The first coil set shows the inverse problem before regularization.

slides.title: Final coils reduce the obvious mismatch
bullets: Compare shape and smoothness
Then ask whether access and curvature remain acceptable
slides.caption: A better coil picture is still not a manufacturability proof.
slides.reference: Ref: SIMSOPT, Landreman et al., JOSS 6, 3525 (2021).

slides.title: B dot n is the basic stage-2 score
bullets: Blue/red patches show normal-field error
The target surface is damaged when patches remain
slides.caption: Quadratic flux measures whether coils reproduce the requested boundary.
slides.reference: Ref: SIMSOPT docs stage-two coil optimization example.

slides.title: Regularization trades field quality for hardware
bullets: Shorter coils can make B dot n worse
A single optimum hides the engineering choice
slides.caption: The annotation marks what gets worse when coils are pushed too hard.

slides.title: Coil complexity has multiple dimensions
bullets: Length: cost, maintenance, and access
Curvature: force and strain risk
Separation: ports and assembly constraints

slides.title: Ports and maintenance enter early
bullets: Access can invalidate attractive coil sets
Use engineering constraints as penalties or hard gates

slides.title: Demo break: SIMSOPT stage-2 coils
bullets: Compare initial and final coils
Plot B dot n maps
Name the tradeoff
slides.caption: Cached mode first. Repo: https://github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization | Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: PART 2. Direct optimization
bullets: Move field, particles, and coils in the same loop
Use differentiable codes where the gradient is meaningful

slides.title: Fieldlines diagnose topology
bullets: Good surfaces support the coil story
Bad topology catches failures a scalar may miss
slides.caption: This cached fieldline is a qualitative diagnostic, not a live ESSOS run.

slides.title: Particles answer a different question
bullets: Fieldlines do not guarantee confinement
Fast-particle gates matter for reactors
slides.caption: Orbit cartoons teach why particle objectives can enter directly.

slides.title: Movie: rotating surface and coils
bullets: Play rotating_surface.gif during class
Use the first frame in static slides
slides.caption: Use the GIF live; use this first-frame PNG when PowerPoint is static.

slides.title: Single-stage design keeps coils in the loop
bullets: Boundary variables and coil variables move together
The objective includes plasma metrics, flux error, and coil metrics
slides.reference: Ref: Jorge et al., PPCF 65, 074003 (2023), single-stage fixed-boundary/coils workflow.

slides.title: Continuation makes the negotiation visible
bullets: Watch objective terms decrease together
Stop when one term dominates the story
slides.caption: A coupled objective is a trace, not just a final number.

slides.title: Weights reveal which constraint is active
bullets: Increasing one weight can improve one metric and hurt another
This is the classroom Pareto intuition
slides.caption: Weight scans are design arguments.

slides.title: Demo break: fieldlines and single-stage toy
bullets: Change one weight
Run cached diagnostics
Defend the result
slides.caption: Cached mode first. Repo: https://github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization | Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: Autodiff still needs validation
bullets: A gradient is a derivative of the implemented model
The implemented model still has a validity domain
Finite differences remain a check

slides.title: Robustness is a stage-2 metric
bullets: Manufacturing errors: sensitivity to shape perturbations
Current perturbations: sensitivity to power-supply changes
Maintenance tolerance: clearance, curvature, and access

slides.title: Lecture 2 what to remember
bullets: Stage 2 turns a field design into hardware
Coil regularization is part of the objective
Single-stage optimization changes the tradeoff surface
Test engineering as a gate

slides.title: Simple coils far away from the plasma are difficult to find
bullets: That sentence is an optimization problem

slides.title: APPENDIX. Lecture 2 checks and replacements
bullets: Use this section when SIMSOPT or ESSOS is available
Keep cached images for lecture timing

slides.title: Stage-2 objective terms
bullets: Normal-field error: does the coil set reproduce the target?
Length and curvature: can the coil be built and maintained?
Separation and access: can the device be assembled and diagnosed?

slides.title: SIMSOPT research path
bullets: Load the public QA target
Build curves and Biot-Savart fields
Run a short optimization before class

slides.title: Fieldlines and particles answer different questions
bullets: Fieldlines: topology and stochasticity
Particles: alpha-loss and orbit width
Both: rerun after coil changes

slides.title: Single-stage acceptance checks
bullets: The plasma metric improves without hiding coil complexity
The coil metric improves without destroying confinement metrics
The combined gradient passes a finite-difference check

slides.title: Backup figure: initial coils
bullets: Use to explain the starting point
Ask what makes the target hard

slides.title: Backup figure: final coils
bullets: Use to explain the claimed improvement
Ask what still needs engineering checks

slides.title: Backup figure: particle orbit
bullets: Use if a particle trace is unavailable
Connect orbit loss to reactor gates

slides.title: Coil robustness outputs to track
bullets: Shape perturbations: sensitivity of B dot n
Current errors: sensitivity of flux surfaces
Mechanical tolerance: clearance, curvature, access

slides.title: Discussion: can a coil set win with the wrong metric?
bullets: Lower B dot n: can come with high curvature
Smoother coils: can miss the target boundary
First experiment: needs both magnetic accuracy and engineering margin

slides.title: What to remember
bullets: Keep the scientific object and the computed artifact together
Rerun, perturb, compare, and explain before trusting the optimum
Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: What should go into the objective function?
slides.subtitle: Lecture 3: neoclassical, turbulence, and fast-particle gates
bullets: Can transport physics become an optimization metric?
Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: PART 1. A hierarchy of transport calculations
bullets: Cheap screens move many designs
Expensive calculations validate finalists

slides.title: The optimizer follows the metric we choose
bullets: Metric design is part of physics

slides.title: Cost hierarchy for transport metrics
bullets: Geometry metrics: fastest screens
Effective ripple and Boozer metrics: early transport warnings
DKE, gyrokinetics, particles, profiles: validation gates

slides.title: Transport literature says: validate the winner
bullets: W7-X: neoclassical optimization reduced the geometry-driven loss channel
SFINCS: drift-kinetic validation depends on trajectory, electric-field, and collision modeling choices
Nonlinear turbulence: heat-flux objectives can be noisy enough to need stochastic or staged optimization
slides.reference: Refs: Beidler et al., Nature 596, 221-226 (2021); Landreman et al., Phys. Plasmas 21, 042503 (2014); Kim et al., JPP 90, 905900203 (2024).

slides.title: Effective ripple is a neoclassical screen
bullets: Rank candidates before expensive runs
Do not treat the scalar as the whole transport story
slides.caption: Cached curves teach the screen; they are not new transport calculations.

slides.title: D11 scans add richer validation
bullets: Collisionality changes the answer
Er suppression changes the interpretation
slides.caption: This SFINCS-style scan is cached educational data.
slides.reference: Ref: Landreman et al., Phys. Plasmas 21, 042503 (2014), SFINCS drift-kinetic solver.

slides.title: Ambipolar roots are not always unique
bullets: Multiple roots imply multiple regimes
Optimization needs a root-choice rule
slides.caption: The root structure is the lesson; the numbers are not a live SFINCS result.

slides.title: Bootstrap current feeds back on equilibrium
bullets: Bootstrap current can alter transform and islands
Profile dependence enters early
slides.caption: Transport outputs become equilibrium inputs.

slides.title: W7-X lesson for optimizers
bullets: Neoclassical optimization: reduced radial losses
Ion-temperature clamping: turbulence bottleneck
Profiles: decide whether the metric matters experimentally

slides.title: Neoclassical success can expose turbulence
bullets: The blue expectation keeps rising
The orange curve saturates
slides.caption: The cartoon motivates turbulence-aware validation without rederiving transport theory.
slides.reference: Ref: Beidler et al., Nature 596, 221-226 (2021); W7-X ion-temperature clamping literature.

slides.title: Demo break: neoclassical cached validation
bullets: Read a collisionality scan
Inspect Er roots
Plot bootstrap feedback
slides.caption: Cached mode first. Repo: https://github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization | Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: PART 2. Turbulence metrics
bullets: Linear calculations are early screens
Nonlinear validation changes rankings

slides.title: Linear growth is a fast warning
bullets: Positive growth marks unstable branches
The peak is not the heat flux
slides.caption: Use growth rate as a screen, not as the final objective.
slides.reference: Ref: SPECTRAX-GK PyPI/docs, JAX-native gyrokinetic solver for stellarator optimization workflows.

slides.title: Frequency helps identify the branch
bullets: Growth and frequency belong together
The sign and trend help classify modes
slides.caption: Frequency is diagnostic context, not the scalar objective.

slides.title: A proxy can pick the wrong winner
bullets: Red circle: proxy winner
Green circle: validation winner
slides.caption: The ranking failure is the point of the plot.
slides.reference: Ref: Kim et al., J. Plasma Phys. 90, 905900203 (2024), nonlinear turbulence optimization with noisy heat fluxes.

slides.title: Demo break: turbulence proxy versus validation
bullets: Choose the proxy winner
Compare validation ranking
Explain the failure mode
slides.caption: Cached mode first. Repo: https://github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization | Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: PART 3. Fast particles are reactor gates
bullets: Alpha confinement, wall loads, and orbit classes matter
Particle metrics must rerun after coil changes

slides.title: Particle metrics connect back to coils
bullets: A fieldline picture can look acceptable
An orbit diagnostic can still fail
slides.caption: Fast-particle checks belong in the validation ladder.

slides.title: Validation compares failure modes
bullets: Does the metric fail on a known bad design?
Does it rank a known good design correctly?
Does it change smoothly under perturbations?

slides.title: Metric trust ladder
bullets: Screen: cached or reduced model trend
Ranking hypothesis: tiny or quasilinear run
Validation: expensive run or experimental comparison

slides.title: Lecture 3 what to remember
bullets: Use cheap metrics to steer the search
Use expensive metrics to challenge finalists
Plot profiles and regimes
Never hide proxy failure

slides.title: Transport metrics are validation gates
bullets: A good design survives a stronger metric

slides.title: APPENDIX. Lecture 3 checks and replacements
bullets: Use this section when SFINCS, SPECTRAX, or particle tools are live
Keep cached ranking plots ready

slides.title: Neoclassical outputs to track
bullets: Effective ripple: low-collisionality screen
Ambipolar roots: radial electric-field validation
Bootstrap current: profile-dependent equilibrium feedback

slides.title: Turbulence outputs to track
bullets: Linear growth rate: fast instability screen
Quasilinear weight: ranking hypothesis
Nonlinear heat flux: validation for finalists

slides.title: Research path: NEO_JAX and SFINCS_JAX
bullets: Choose the same equilibrium and flux surface
Scan collisionality and radial electric field
Compare compact metrics before detailed validation

slides.title: Research path: SPECTRAX-GK
bullets: Start with a tiny linear calculation
Plot growth rate and frequency versus ky
Keep nonlinear validation gate explicit

slides.title: How to read a fast metric
bullets: Growth rate screens instability before heat-flux validation
A scalar ripple value should point back to where losses occur
A profile proxy should lead to a solved discharge model

slides.title: Backup figure: growth-rate spectrum
bullets: Use if a live linear run is slow
Ask what the scalar should be

slides.title: Backup figure: W7-X clamping cartoon
bullets: Use to motivate turbulence after neoclassical success
Keep the cartoon label visible

slides.title: Before showing a transport number
bullets: Equilibrium: which surface and assumptions?
Kinetics: which species and collisionality?
Validation: which expensive calculation confirms it?

slides.title: Discussion: what can reverse the ranking?
bullets: Neoclassical winner: can lose on turbulence
Turbulence winner: can fail alpha confinement
Profile change: can move both metrics together

slides.title: What to remember
bullets: Keep the scientific object and the computed artifact together
Rerun, perturb, compare, and explain before trusting the optimum
Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: When does an optimized field become a reactor design?
slides.subtitle: Lecture 4: profiles, Pareto decisions, and the repo lab
bullets: A design becomes credible when the workflow is validated
Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: The full loop has memory
bullets: Geometry changes transport
Transport changes profiles
Profiles change objectives

slides.title: Modern workflows are multi-fidelity by design
bullets: SIMSOPT: objective terms, geometry objects, and coils live in one optimization framework
JAX transport tools: differentiable metrics are useful only inside a documented claim scope
Profile closure: sources and turbulence can reverse a geometry-only ranking
slides.reference: Refs: Landreman et al., JOSS 6, 3525 (2021); sfincs-jax and SPECTRAX-GK public docs; Kim et al., JPP 90, 905900203 (2024).

slides.title: PART 1. Close the loop with profiles
bullets: Field optimization changes transport
Transport changes pressure and current

slides.title: Profiles turn a field optimum into a scenario
bullets: Temperature and density gradients drive transport
A good field metric can underperform after closure
slides.caption: This fallback model teaches profile feedback, not a live NEOPAX result.
slides.reference: Ref: transport/profile closure logic in nonlinear turbulence optimization and T3D validation literature.

slides.title: Power balance is a credibility check
bullets: Sources, fluxes, and residuals must match
A post-hoc residual is weak evidence
slides.caption: A design claim needs a solved balance, not only a field metric.

slides.title: Integrated objectives should be staged
bullets: Early: cheap geometry and Boozer screens
Middle: coils and neoclassical metrics
Late: turbulence, particles, profiles, engineering

slides.title: Demo break: profile closure
bullets: Plot temperature response
Inspect power balance
Say what remains cached
slides.caption: Cached mode first. Repo: https://github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization | Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: PART 2. Pareto decisions
bullets: Many designs can be defensible
The decision weights must be explicit

slides.title: Reactor constraints enter as gates
bullets: Alpha heating and losses: reactor-relevant loss gate
Wall loads and blankets: engineering and material constraint
Maintenance and access: device availability constraint

slides.title: Pareto fronts need interpretation
bullets: Nondominated points still require priorities
Final choice needs context and validation
slides.caption: A Pareto front is an argument surface, not an automatic answer.
slides.reference: Ref: SIMSOPT objective composition and multiobjective stellarator optimization workflows.

slides.title: Weights expose the design decision
bullets: Changing weights changes the winner
Record weights beside every selected design
slides.caption: A weighted score is a value judgment with units and provenance.

slides.title: Demo break: Pareto dashboard
bullets: Change one metric weight
Regenerate the chart
Defend the selected design
slides.caption: Cached mode first. Repo: https://github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization | Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: Validation gates prevent metric gaming
bullets: Equilibrium, Boozer, neoclassical, turbulence, particles, profiles, coils, engineering
A design can pass one gate and fail the next

slides.title: PART 3. The GitHub repo is the lab manual
bullets: Slides explain concepts
Notebooks execute projects
Scripts regenerate figures, movies, and status

slides.title: A design is a validated workflow
bullets: The repo is the audit trail

slides.title: Repo lab flow
bullets: python scripts/check_release_ready.py
python scripts/make_lecture_bundle.py
python scripts/audit_notebook_outputs.py

slides.title: Cached, tiny, and research modes serve different audiences
bullets: cached: student reliability
tiny: instructor live demo
research: real-code replacement after timing

slides.title: ReadTheDocs is the canonical guide
bullets: Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/
Repo: github.com/rogeriojorge/sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization

Review STATUS.md
Open rendered docs

slides.title: Coil robustness returns in the final workflow
bullets: Manufacturing tolerance: can the design survive perturbations?
Current errors: can operations maintain the target field?
Thermal and mechanical deformation: does the coil story remain true?

slides.title: There is no single best stellarator
bullets: There are only defensible choices with stated priorities

slides.title: Exercise: change the weights and defend a design
bullets: Move one weight
Identify the new winner
Say which validation gate you now trust less
slides.caption: Cached dashboard first. Docs: https://sos2026-rjorge-stellarator-optimization.readthedocs.io/

slides.title: Documentation is part of reproducibility
bullets: README: how a student starts
ReadTheDocs: the rendered guide during class
STATUS.md: what was real, cached, or synthetic

slides.title: APPENDIX. Lecture 4 checks and replacements
bullets: Use this section before distributing the school bundle
Keep cached mode honest

slides.title: What belongs in git
bullets: Small inputs: versioned and documented
Cached data: labeled educational fallback
Large outputs: regenerated by scripts and ignored

slides.title: What stays out of git
bullets: Original package PDFs and PPTX files
Private screenshots and huge generated media
Local environments and secrets

slides.title: STATUS.md anatomy
bullets: Package status: what imported and what was skipped
Data status: what was real and what was synthetic
Notebook status: what ran and saved outputs

slides.title: Live-demo abort criteria
bullets: A package starts compiling during class
A notebook loses its cached fallback
A result is numerical but the validation path is missing

slides.title: Movie: toy optimization history
bullets: Play optimization_history.gif for the loop intuition
Use first frame for PDF/static review
slides.caption: Use the GIF live; use this first-frame PNG when PowerPoint is static.

slides.title: Manual steps before the school
bullets: Choose the lecture machine and environment
Pre-fetch equilibria and verify notebooks render
Freeze the branch or tag used for the school

slides.title: How students should use the repo after school
bullets: Start cached: reproduce every figure
Change one notebook at a time
Keep generated outputs small and documented

slides.title: Backup figure: profile closure
bullets: Use if live profile closure fails
Keep the cached limitation explicit
slides.caption: Fallback plot for profile closure discussion.

slides.title: Backup figure: Pareto front
bullets: Use if dashboard edits run long
Ask which design the room would defend

slides.title: Final design commandments
bullets: Show every boundary with its coil story
State the validation domain for every metric
Pair each proxy with its failure mode
Show the weights behind each Pareto choice

slides.title: Coupled, differentiable, reproducible stellarator design
